meta.title: Derive in render vs store derived state in React
meta.summary: A React filter panel needs visible results, a count badge, and a disabled Apply button. Would you compute these from current state or store them separately?

scenario: You are building a React product filter panel. The component already stores the source values: the raw product list, the current search text, the selected category, and which items the user has selected. The UI also needs a filtered result list, a visible count badge, and a disabled Apply button when nothing matches. In the interview, the panel asks whether you would calculate those values directly during render from the current source state or store them as separate state and keep them in sync.
prompt: Which direction would you defend for this prompt, and how would you explain when the other option is still the better answer?

options.label: Derive in render
options.summary: Keeps one source of truth when a value can be recomputed from the current state and props.
whenItWins: The value is fully determined by current inputs such as the product list, search term, selected category, or selected items.
You want to avoid sync bugs where the stored derived value falls out of date with the real source state.
The computation is clear enough to read directly or memoize, without inventing another state variable for something React can already compute.
watchOutFor: If the computation is genuinely heavy, you may need memoization, but it is still usually better to memoize a derivation than to duplicate state blindly.
A weak answer says 'derive everything' without noticing when the product really needs a committed snapshot or separate lifecycle.

options.label: Store derived state separately
options.summary: Can make sense when the value is no longer just a live calculation and now needs its own lifecycle as a snapshot or committed result.
whenItWins: The product needs a value that intentionally diverges from the live inputs, such as an applied snapshot that should not update until the user confirms again.
The state is no longer purely derived because the user can stage one set of inputs and commit another set later.
You can clearly explain why this is a separate source of truth instead of a duplicated copy of something React could already recalculate.
watchOutFor: If the value is still just a pure calculation from current inputs, storing it separately creates more sync work and more bug surface.
A weak answer stores filtered lists, counts, or booleans in state just because it feels easier, then adds effects and setters to keep the copies aligned.

decisionMatrix.title: Pure UI derivation
decisionMatrix.prompt: Filtered items, visible count, and disabled button can all be recomputed from the current source state on every render.

cells.note: This is the easiest answer to defend because the values already fall out of the current state without needing another source of truth.
You can make it work, but now you are paying sync cost for values React could already calculate.

decisionMatrix.title: The exact prompt in this battle
decisionMatrix.prompt: The component needs filtered results, a count badge, and an Apply button state based on the current inputs right now.

cells.note: This keeps the logic tied to the real source state and avoids extra synchronization code.
Possible, but only if you can justify a separate lifecycle. In this prompt that reason is weak.

decisionMatrix.title: Committed or applied snapshot
decisionMatrix.prompt: Draft filters can change live, but the results should only update after the user presses Apply.

cells.note: Still viable if you derive from committed state instead of draft state, but you now need a clearer boundary between draft and applied values.
This becomes much easier to defend when the stored value is intentionally a committed snapshot rather than a duplicated live calculation.

evaluationDimensions.title: Is there already one clear source of truth?
evaluationDimensions.description: If the answer is yes, deriving in render usually keeps the code cleaner because you are not creating another copy to maintain.

evaluationDimensions.title: How likely is duplicated state to drift out of sync?
evaluationDimensions.description: Every extra stored copy is one more place that can become stale when the real source values change.

evaluationDimensions.title: Do you need a live calculation or a committed snapshot?
evaluationDimensions.description: This is the key split. Pure live calculations want derivation. Committed snapshots may deserve their own state.

evaluationDimensions.title: Is the calculation expensive enough to optimize?
evaluationDimensions.description: If cost becomes a problem, memoization is often the first move. Performance alone is not a good reason to duplicate state by default.

evaluationDimensions.title: Will the next developer understand why this value exists?
evaluationDimensions.description: A strong answer should make it obvious whether a value is derived from current inputs or represents a separate product state on purpose.

strongAnswer.title: A strong answer here would lean derive in render because these values already come from the current source state
strongAnswer.summary: Filtered results, visible count, and disabled-button state are all pure calculations from the current inputs in this prompt. That makes derive-in-render the clearer answer.
reasoning: The component already stores the real source values. If I add filtered results and count as separate state too, I create more sync work without adding new meaning.
Derived values are safer when they are computed from the latest source state because there is no second copy to forget to update.
If performance becomes a real issue, I would first consider memoizing the derivation. I would only store the value separately if the product needed a committed snapshot with its own lifecycle.
strongAnswer.recommendation: For this exact prompt, I would derive the filtered list, visible count, and disabled Apply state from the current source values during render, and only introduce separate stored state if the product later needs a true draft-versus-applied boundary.

interviewerPushback.question: Why not just put filteredResults in state so it is easier to use?
interviewerPushback.answer: Because filteredResults is still only a calculation from the real source state. Storing it separately makes me responsible for keeping two sources aligned, which usually adds bugs instead of clarity.

interviewerPushback.question: What if recalculating on every render is expensive?
interviewerPushback.answer: Then I would first ask whether it is actually a measured problem. If it is, I would look at memoization or the shape of the computation before duplicating the value into extra state.

interviewerPushback.question: What would change your answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: If the product needed a committed snapshot, such as draft filters that should not affect results until Apply is pressed, I would become much more comfortable storing separate state because that value would now represent a different lifecycle, not just a repeated calculation.

answerExamples.title: Weak answer
answerExamples.answer: I would store filteredResults in state because it is easier.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: It does not. This ignores sync risk and does not explain why the value deserves its own state instead of being derived from the real source values.

answerExamples.title: Decent answer
answerExamples.answer: I would probably derive it in render because it comes from other state already.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This is directionally fine, but it still needs a clearer explanation of single source of truth, sync bugs, and when stored derived state becomes valid.

answerExamples.title: Strong answer
answerExamples.answer: For this prompt I would derive the filtered list, count badge, and disabled Apply state from the current source values during render because they are all pure calculations from state I already have. Storing them separately would create duplicate state and extra sync work. If the product later introduced a real draft-versus-applied flow, I would revisit that and consider separate stored state for the committed snapshot.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This sounds strong because it makes the single-source-of-truth argument clearly, explains the sync risk, and still gives a fair boundary for when storing the value separately is justified.

answerFramework: Start by naming the real source values the component already owns.
Ask whether the extra value is a pure live calculation or a separate committed snapshot.
State your choice for this prompt and explain the sync cost of duplicating state.
Mention memoization if performance is the real concern instead of jumping straight to extra state.
End with a clean boundary, for example: derive live UI values, store only states that truly have their own lifecycle.
antiPatterns: Copying filtered lists, counts, or booleans into state by default just because the value is used in more than one place.
Using useEffect only to keep duplicated derived state synchronized with the real source values.
Treating performance as a reason to duplicate state before measuring whether the computation is actually a problem.
Ignoring the difference between a live derivation and a committed snapshot.
